env_note: All tasks assume a local single-node cluster set up with minikube or kind. kubectl must be configured to point to that cluster. To start minikube: `minikube start`.

tasks.title: Warm-up: Fill in the YAML Skeleton
tasks.prompt:
The manifest below is for a Pod named 'hello-pod', but some of the required fields are missing. Fill in the blanks (_____), apply the manifest with `kubectl apply -f hello-pod.yaml`, and confirm that `kubectl get pod hello-pod` shows a STATUS of Running.

```yaml
_____: v1          # Pod belongs to the core API group
kind: _____
metadata:
  name: hello-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: hello
      image: _____
```
tasks.starter:
# hello-pod.yaml (fill in the blanks)
_____: v1
kind: _____
metadata:
  name: hello-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: hello
      image: _____
tasks.solution:
# hello-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: hello-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: hello
      image: nginx:1.27
tasks.explanation:
`apiVersion` depends on the resource type. Pods and Services belong to the Kubernetes core API group, so they use `v1`. `kind` is the type of resource you want to create — write `Pod` exactly as shown (case-sensitive). `image` must refer to a publicly available image. Here we use the official `nginx:1.27` image from Docker Hub.

After applying, run `kubectl get pod hello-pod -w` to watch the STATUS change in real time. It should transition from ContainerCreating to Running. To clean up: `kubectl delete pod hello-pod`.
hints: Every manifest requires four top-level fields: `apiVersion`, `kind`, `metadata`, and `spec`. The first blank is one of those fields.
The `apiVersion` for `kind: Pod` is `v1`. Remember: Deployments use `apps/v1`, while Pods and Services use `v1`.
Images are written in the format `image-name:tag`, for example `nginx:1.27`. Omitting the tag (just `nginx`) is valid, but specifying a version tag is standard practice in production.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 1: Write, Apply, and Verify a Deployment YAML
tasks.prompt:
Write a Deployment manifest (`my-deployment.yaml`) from scratch that meets the following requirements and apply it to the cluster.

Requirements:
- Name: `my-nginx`
- Replicas: 3
- Container name: `nginx-container`, image: `nginx:1.27`, container port: 80
- Pod label: `app: my-nginx`

After applying, verify the state with the commands below.
```bash
kubectl get deployment my-nginx
kubectl get pods -l app=my-nginx
```

Expected result: READY column shows `3/3`, and all 3 Pods are in Running status.
tasks.starter:
# my-deployment.yaml — write your manifest below
# Hint: start with the four top-level fields (apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec)
tasks.solution:
# my-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-nginx
  labels:
    app: my-nginx
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-nginx
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: nginx-container
          image: nginx:1.27
          ports:
            - containerPort: 80
tasks.explanation:
The most important rule in a Deployment manifest is that `spec.selector.matchLabels` and `spec.template.metadata.labels` must match exactly. The Deployment controller uses this label selector to identify which Pods it manages, so a mismatch will cause an error at `kubectl apply` time.

`kubectl get pods -l app=my-nginx` uses the `-l` (label selector) flag to show only Pods with that label. Also notice that all three Pod names follow the pattern `my-nginx-<hash>-<hash>` — these names are auto-generated by the Deployment through its ReplicaSet.

To clean up: `kubectl delete deployment my-nginx`
hints: The `apiVersion` for a Deployment is `apps/v1`. Using just `v1` will produce a `no kind 'Deployment' is registered...` error.
Make sure the label values under `spec.selector.matchLabels` and `spec.template.metadata.labels` are identical. If they differ, Kubernetes will reject the manifest immediately.
The `containers` field is a YAML list, so each entry starts with a `-` (hyphen). Use 2 spaces per indent level — tab characters are not allowed.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 2: Write a Service YAML and Verify nginx Responds
tasks.prompt:
With the `my-nginx` Deployment from Core Exercise 1 still running, write a NodePort Service manifest (`my-service.yaml`) and apply it to the cluster.

Requirements:
- Name: `my-nginx-svc`
- Type: NodePort
- Selector label: `app: my-nginx`
- port: 80, targetPort: 80, nodePort: 30090

After applying, verify with the commands below. If you are using minikube, confirm the response with curl.
```bash
kubectl get service my-nginx-svc
# Verify connectivity in a minikube environment
curl http://$(minikube ip):30090
```

Success is confirmed when `Welcome to nginx!` appears in the response.
tasks.starter:
# my-service.yaml — write your manifest below
# Write fields in the order: apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec
# You will need spec.type, spec.selector, and spec.ports
tasks.solution:
# my-service.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: my-nginx-svc
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: my-nginx
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 80
      nodePort: 30090
tasks.explanation:
The `apiVersion` for a Service is `v1` (not `apps/v1` like a Deployment). The label in `spec.selector` (`app: my-nginx`) must match the label the Deployment placed on its Pods. This label match determines which Pods the Service forwards traffic to.

For NodePort Services, here is what each port field does:
- `port: 80` — the port the Service listens on within the cluster (via its ClusterIP)
- `targetPort: 80` — the port the actual container (Pod) is listening on
- `nodePort: 30090` — the port exposed on each node for external access (must be in the range 30000–32767)

If `kubectl get service my-nginx-svc` shows `80:30090/TCP` in the PORT(S) column, the Service is configured correctly.

In a minikube environment, `minikube ip` returns the node IP (typically in the 192.168.x.x range), and nginx is accessible on port 30090 of that IP.

To clean up: `kubectl delete -f my-service.yaml -f my-deployment.yaml`
hints: The `apiVersion` for a Service is `v1`. If you omit `spec.type: NodePort`, the Service defaults to ClusterIP type and will not be reachable from outside the cluster.
The label value in `spec.selector` must exactly match `app: my-nginx` as defined in Core Exercise 1. Even a single typo will prevent traffic from reaching the Pods.

If curl returns no response or a `Connection refused` error, run `kubectl get endpoints my-nginx-svc`. If the ENDPOINTS column shows Pod IPs, the Service is connected to the Pods. If it shows `<none>`, there is a label selector mismatch.

tasks.title: Advanced: Scale Up Replicas and Observe a Zero-Downtime Update
tasks.prompt:
Edit `my-deployment.yaml` from Core Exercise 1 and change `replicas` from 3 to 5, then run `kubectl apply` again. The key point is to modify only the file and re-apply — do not delete the existing cluster resources.

Steps:
1. Edit `my-deployment.yaml` and change `replicas: 3` to `replicas: 5`
2. Run `kubectl apply -f my-deployment.yaml`
3. Verify that the Pod count has increased to 5

```bash
kubectl get deployment my-nginx
kubectl get pods -l app=my-nginx
```

Expected result: READY `5/5`, with 5 Running Pods.

Bonus challenge: Force-delete one Pod with `kubectl delete pod <pod-name>`, wait a moment, then run `kubectl get pods -l app=my-nginx` again and confirm that Kubernetes automatically recovers to 5 Pods.
tasks.starter:
# File to edit: my-deployment.yaml (created in Core Exercise 1)
# Change only the replicas field from 3 to 5 and save
# Before:
#   replicas: 3
# After:
#   replicas: 5
tasks.solution:
# my-deployment.yaml (final version with updated replicas)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-nginx
  labels:
    app: my-nginx
spec:
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-nginx
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: nginx-container
          image: nginx:1.27
          ports:
            - containerPort: 80

# Apply command
# kubectl apply -f my-deployment.yaml

# Verification commands
# kubectl get deployment my-nginx
# kubectl get pods -l app=my-nginx

# Self-healing verification (replace pod name with your actual pod name)
# kubectl delete pod <my-nginx-xxx-yyy>
# kubectl get pods -l app=my-nginx   # Run again after a moment — should still show 5 Pods
tasks.explanation:
This advanced exercise lets you experience two core advantages of the declarative approach firsthand.

First, **idempotency**: `kubectl apply` compares the file's state to the cluster's current state and applies only the differences. Changing `replicas: 3` to `replicas: 5` tells Kubernetes to keep the existing 3 Pods and add 2 new ones. The scale-out happens without any service interruption.

Second, **self-healing**: The Deployment controller continuously works through its reconciliation loop to maintain the declared goal of 5 replicas. When you force-delete a Pod, the controller detects the shortfall immediately and creates a new Pod to restore the count to 5. This is the core reason Deployments are used in production environments.

Watch the `UP-TO-DATE` value in `kubectl get deployment my-nginx` change to 5, and wait for `AVAILABLE` to reach 5 as well.

Full cleanup: `kubectl delete deployment my-nginx && kubectl delete service my-nginx-svc`
hints: Only change the `replicas: 3` line to `replicas: 5`. No other fields need to be modified. Save the file, then run `kubectl apply -f my-deployment.yaml`.
Right after running `kubectl apply`, run `kubectl get pods -l app=my-nginx -w` to watch the new Pods transition from ContainerCreating to Running in real time (press Ctrl+C to stop watching).

If you don't know the Pod name, run `kubectl get pods -l app=my-nginx` to find one, copy any Pod name, and delete it with `kubectl delete pod <name>`. After about 5–10 seconds, querying again should show a new Pod in Running status, keeping the total at 5.
